---
title: "ISIC-2024 SLICE-3D"
subtitle: "An efficiency-frontier, single-dataset, no-external-data study of skin-lesion classification"
pagetitle: "ISIC-2024 SLICE-3D · Pakistan.AI"
author:
  - name: "Raja, Muhammad Junaid Ali Asif"
    note: "M11217073"
  - name: "Sultan, Adil"
    note: "M11217078"
  - name: "Hassan, Shahzaib Ahmed"
    note: "M11217081"
date: last-modified
toc: true
---

::: {.hero}
[Pakistan.AI · Neural Networks · CSIE, NYUST]{.eyebrow}

# ISIC-2024 SLICE-3D — the honest frontier

Detecting the **393 malignant lesions hidden among 400,666 benign crops** (a 1,021:1
imbalance) — using **only** the ISIC-2024 SLICE-3D dataset, **no** external dermoscopy,
and **no** synthetic/generative positives.

::: {.claim}
**The claim under test:** *state-of-the-art among single-dataset, no-external-data,
no-synthetic solutions — reported not as one number, but as a **quality-vs-cost Pareto frontier**.*
:::
:::

::: {.stat-cards}
::: {.stat-card .is-feature}
[0.1743]{.stat-value}
[Stack OOF pAUC@80%TPR]{.stat-label}
:::
::: {.stat-card}
[0.1689]{.stat-value}
[Tabular GBDT OOF pAUC]{.stat-label}
:::
::: {.stat-card}
[0.098[%]{.unit}]{.stat-value}
[Malignant prevalence]{.stat-label}
:::
::: {.stat-card}
[401[k]{.unit}]{.stat-value}
[Lesion crops, 1 dataset]{.stat-label}
:::
::: {.stat-card}
[12]{.stat-value}
[Backbones on the frontier]{.stat-label}
:::
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" icon=false}
## On this site
This is the complete companion report for the project. It is written to be read by anyone —
from someone meeting the partial-AUC metric for the first time, to a reviewer checking that
every headline number is reproducible from disk.

- **[Data & EDA](data.qmd)** — the dataset, the imbalance, the nine exploratory figures, the leak columns we drop.
- **[Methods](methods.qmd)** — basic → complex: the metric, the CV spine, tabular features, the GBDT, the image experts, the stack.
- **[Results](results.qmd)** — the three-axis Pareto frontier, ROC / pAUC region, per-fold, calibration.
- **[Ablations](ablations.qmd)** — every complexity increase we tried, including the ones that lost (that *is* the finding).
- **[Reproduce](reproducibility.qmd)** — the exact env, seeds, and commands; how each number was re-verified.
- **[Credits](credits.qmd)** — gracious credit to the leaderboard champions and the public-notebook lineage.
- **[References](references.qmd)** — full citations; dataset, metric, backbones, GBDTs.

**Offline copies:** [report PDF](ISIC2024-report.pdf) · [slide deck (HTML)](slides.qmd)
:::

## Abstract

We study the **ISIC-2024 SLICE-3D** skin-lesion task — classifying ~401,059 lesion crops
extracted from 3D total-body photography (TBP) as malignant or benign — under a deliberately
strict regime. The competition's top private-leaderboard solutions reached pAUC ≈ 0.173, but
they did so by importing **external** dermoscopy archives **and** roughly **30,000
diffusion-synthesized malignant lesions**. We ban both. On medical images, training or
validating on fabricated pathology is a label-validity hole; and importing external data
breaks the "single dataset" premise. Removing those two crutches is exactly what our
experiment isolates.

Because the absolute leaderboard number is then **out of reach by construction**, we re-pose
the problem as a **Pareto frontier**: the best official **pAUC above 80% TPR** achievable per
unit of inference cost (parameters, FLOPs, and single-thread CPU latency), on SLICE-3D data
alone. The architecture is a **hybrid**: a LightGBM tabular expert over intrinsic engineered
features (geometry, color/hue contrast, size ratios, and patient-relative "ugly-duckling"
deviations) provides the efficient anchor; a **small** ImageNet-pretrained image backbone
contributes an out-of-fold (OOF) malignancy probability that is fused into the final score by
a **trivial rank-average** combiner.

The honest punchline: **every increase in model complexity we tried lost.** The trivial
2-way rank-average beats a meta-LightGBM stacker, a learned per-lesion gate, and the addition
of image embeddings; the small `convnextv2_nano` backbone beats heavy transformers
(SwinV2, EVA-02) that collapse to near-random at 393 positives. Our best frontier point —
`rank-avg[GBDT, convnextv2_nano@224]` — reaches **OOF pAUC 0.17430** at **15.8 M params /
2.46 GFLOPs / 61 ms CPU per image**.

## Headline results {#headline}

All numbers are **out-of-fold (OOF)** on the frozen patient-grouped folds
(`data/folds.parquet`, 5 folds, SEED = 42), and every pAUC is computed **only** by the
official scorer (`src/cv.py`), pinned at **80% TPR** (range `[0, 0.20]`; random ≈ 0.02,
perfect = 0.20). All values were independently re-derived from the OOF parquet files by the
`cv-guardian` agent.

| Model | OOF pAUC@80%TPR | Params (M) | GFLOPs | CPU ms/img | Role |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|
| **GBDT (tabular only)** | **0.16890** | 0.86 | ~0 | 0.02 | the efficient anchor — ≈ free |
| Best image (`convnextv2_nano@224`) | 0.15945 | 14.98 | 2.46 | 60.9 | the protagonist backbone |
| **Stack — `rank-avg[GBDT, nano@224]`** | **[0.17430]{.metric-chip}** | 15.84 | 2.46 | 60.9 | best overall; **Pareto-optimal** |
| Cheap stack (`gbdt+3img+udk`) | 0.17117 | 23.84 | 1.22 | 32.9 | also on the frontier (cheaper) |
| Frontier — cheap end (`effvit_b0`) | 0.13706 | 2.13 | 0.034 | 3.5 | most accuracy per millisecond |
| Frontier — cheapest (`mnv4_small`) | 0.11242 | 2.49 | 0.062 | 3.1 | floor of the cost axis |

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
**Reading the metric.** pAUC@80%TPR integrates the ROC curve only in the high-sensitivity tail
(TPR ≥ 0.80). It is bounded in `[0, 0.20]`, so a move from 0.169 → 0.174 is a **+3.2% relative**
improvement, not a rounding error. See [Methods → The metric](methods.qmd#the-metric).
:::

### Where the score comes from

The single most important finding for *understanding* the task: the dominant signal is not the
raw lesion, it is **how anomalous a lesion is relative to the same patient's other moles** —
the classic "ugly-duckling" sign. Patient-relative features (`pdev_*`, `prank_*`, `pxc_*`)
account for **~65% of total GBDT gain**. The top features:

| Rank | Feature | Mean gain % | What it is |
|---:|---|---:|---|
| 1 | `tbp_lv_H` | 3.28 | lesion hue angle (univariate AUC 0.81 on its own) |
| 2 | `pdev_tbp_lv_H` | 3.05 | how far this lesion's hue deviates from the patient's mean |
| 3 | `pdev_clin_size_long_diam_mm` | 2.19 | size deviation vs the patient's own lesions |
| 4 | `pxc_tbp_lv_H_location` | 1.88 | hue × body-location patient interaction |

## The pipeline

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-pipeline
%%| fig-cap: "End-to-end pipeline. Patient-grouped folds are frozen once; every expert and the stack read the same split. Nothing trains until the no-leak guarantee passes."
flowchart TD
    A["ISIC-2024 SLICE-3D<br/>401,059 crops · 1,042 patients<br/>393 malignant / 400,666 benign"] --> B

    B["Patient-grouped<br/>StratifiedGroupKFold<br/>(5 folds · SEED=42 · frozen)<br/><b>no patient straddles folds</b>"]

    B --> C["Tabular feature engineering<br/>geometry · color/hue · size ratios<br/>3D position · patient-relative<br/>ugly-duckling (pdev / prank / pxc)<br/>per-fold smoothed target encoding"]
    B --> E["Image crops @128 / @224<br/>small ImageNet-pretrained backbone<br/>per-epoch neg-undersample ~1:1<br/>BCE+LS · EMA(0.995) · mixup · TTA"]

    C --> D["Bagged GBDT expert<br/>LightGBM (5-seed) + CatBoost<br/>rank-blend<br/><b>OOF pAUC 0.16890</b>"]
    E --> F["Image expert<br/>OOF malignancy prob<br/>(+ embedding, ablated)<br/><b>OOF pAUC 0.15945</b>"]

    D --> G["Combiner = rank-average<br/>(param-free, robust)"]
    F --> G

    G --> H["Final OOF score<br/><b>pAUC 0.17430</b><br/>15.8M params · 2.46 GFLOPs · 61 ms CPU"]
    H --> I["Submission / frontier point"]

    style A fill:#0b6e4f,color:#fff
    style B fill:#117a65,color:#fff
    style H fill:#1e8449,color:#fff
    style I fill:#073b2c,color:#fff
```

## Scope honesty {#scope-honesty}

::: {.callout-important}
## What we are — and are not — claiming
- We are **not** claiming to beat the unconstrained leaderboard winner's private pAUC ≈ 0.1755.
  That score used external data + ~30k synthetic positives; matching it under our rules is
  **impossible by construction**, and that is precisely the point of the experiment.
- We **are** claiming SOTA *within the no-external-data / no-synthetic / single-dataset class*,
  on a transparent quality-vs-cost frontier, with leak-audited CV.
- The 1st-place team's own ablation shows their ~30k synthetic lesions added only **+0.0007**
  pAUC — a sobering data point for the "just add synthetic data" reflex on a 393-positive task.
- Our OOF number is, if anything, a **more honest** predictor of generalization than a
  leaderboard-tuned number, because no part of our pipeline ever saw out-of-distribution data.
  Expect a typical public→private drop of **~0.013–0.021** on this task; we report the OOF CV
  and state that projection openly.
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
## Licensing note on figures
Every figure on this site is **our own** (generated by `reports/eda/make_eda.py`,
`reports/perf/make_perf.py`, `reports/frontier.py`) plus Mermaid diagrams. We deliberately use
**no external copyrighted images** — appropriate for a medical-imaging deliverable and for the
dataset's CC BY-NC 4.0 terms.
:::

---

*Continue to [Data & EDA →](data.qmd)*
